content.problemStatement:
- The **Optimal Page Replacement Algorithm** (OPT) is the most efficient page replacement strategy — but with a catch: it's not practically implementable for real-time use.
- In OPT, **when a page needs to be replaced**, the algorithm removes the **page that will not be used for the longest time in the future**.
- It’s like having a magical crystal ball that tells the OS exactly which page won’t be needed soon, so that one gets removed!

---

**Real-Life Analogy:**
Imagine you’re packing for a trip and your bag has limited space. If you somehow know that you won’t wear your black jeans until the last day of the trip, you can safely pack them at the bottom or even leave them out.



That’s what OPT does—it removes the page that won't be used for the longest time ahead.

---

**Example:**
Let’s say we have **3 memory frames**, and the following **page requests** arrive:
**`Page requests: 7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4`**

We’ll apply OPT and track page faults.
| **Step** | **Request** | **Memory Frames** | **Page Fault?** | **Action Taken**               |
| ---- | ------- | ------------- | ----------- | -------------------------- |
| 1    | 7       | \[7]          | ✅ Yes       | Loaded 7                   |
| 2    | 0       | \[7, 0]       | ✅ Yes       | Loaded 0                   |
| 3    | 1       | \[7, 0, 1]    | ✅ Yes       | Loaded 1                   |
| 4    | 2       | \[0, 1, 2]    | ✅ Yes       | 7 removed (used latest)    |
| 5    | 0       | \[0, 1, 2]    | ❌ No        | Already in memory          |
| 6    | 3       | \[0, 2, 3]    | ✅ Yes       | 1 removed (used farthest)  |
| 7    | 0       | \[0, 2, 3]    | ❌ No        | Already in memory          |
| 8    | 4       | \[0, 3, 4]    | ✅ Yes       | 2 removed (not used again) |

- **Total Page Faults: 6**
- **Better than** FIFO and LRU for the same input

---

**OPT** replaces the page that will **not** be used for the **longest time** in the future. It gives the **lowest possible number** of page faults.

**Not practical in real systems** because the OS cannot predict future requests. It is used as a benchmark to compare how other algorithms perform.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
